statusLine: EU fuel stocks are under pressure in June 2026, with jet fuel reserves below mandatory minimums across the majority of member states and petrol stocks critically low in over half the bloc.
fullAnalysis:
As of mid-June 2026, the EU's aggregate fuel stock picture masks significant strain at the member-state level. The bloc-wide averages — petrol at 95.6 days, diesel at 88.1 days, and jet fuel at 87 days — sit close to or just above the 90-day mandatory minimum for net imports, but these figures are skewed upward by a handful of well-stocked northern and Baltic states. At the country level, the situation is considerably more acute: 20 of 27 member states hold petrol reserves below the 90-day threshold, 18 of 27 are below minimum on diesel, and jet fuel is the most exposed product, with 19 of 27 countries rated critical on that metric alone.

The most severely affected countries are clustered in Central and Southern Europe. Romania's diesel stock stands at just 30.2 days — less than one-third of the required minimum. Poland's jet fuel reserve is 22.5 days, and Portugal's is 21.2 days, both alarmingly thin for major aviation hubs. Luxembourg and Austria hold critical-rated stocks across all three fuel categories. These deficits are occurring against the backdrop of recent strategic reserve releases prompted by Middle East supply disruptions, which suggests that some of the current shortfalls reflect drawdowns that have not yet been replenished. Brent crude has risen 11.2% since late February, reaching $80.59 per barrel, which increases the cost of rebuilding those reserves and may be discouraging faster restocking.

Fuel prices reflect a patchwork of national tax regimes and subsidy policies more than a direct read on local supply tightness. Denmark leads petrol prices at €2.392 per litre, while Malta's €1.34 and Poland's €1.408 sit at the low end — the latter despite Poland holding critically low stocks across all categories, likely reflecting domestic pricing interventions. The EU petrol average of €1.771 and diesel at €1.781 per litre are consistent with the elevated crude environment. Germany and the Nordic states — Sweden, Finland, Denmark, Estonia — show more resilient stock positions overall, with several holding safe-rated petrol and diesel reserves, though Finland's jet fuel stands at only 31.9 days, a notable weak point for a country otherwise well-stocked.

The jet fuel picture warrants particular attention as summer aviation demand peaks. With 19 of 27 member states in a critical position on jet fuel and the EU average at 87 days — below the 90-day floor — any further supply disruption or demand surge could translate quickly into operational pressure for airlines and airports. Countries with large aviation sectors, including France (65 days), Italy (61.5 days), Spain (35.6 days), and the Netherlands (63.2 days), are all carrying jet fuel stocks well below minimum levels. Policymakers should monitor restocking rates closely in the coming weeks, particularly if crude prices continue their upward trajectory.
keyPoints: 20 of 27 EU member states hold petrol stocks below the mandatory 90-day minimum, and 19 of 27 are critically low on jet fuel — the most exposed product across the bloc.
Romania (diesel: 30.2 days), Portugal (jet fuel: 21.2 days), and Poland (jet fuel: 22.5 days) represent the most severe individual shortfalls, all well under one-third of the required minimum.

The EU jet fuel average of 87 days falls below the 90-day mandatory threshold, with major aviation markets France, Spain, Italy, and the Netherlands all holding critically low reserves during peak summer travel season.

Brent crude has risen 11.2% since late February to $80.59 per barrel, raising the cost of reserve replenishment at the same time that several countries have already drawn down strategic stocks in response to Middle East supply disruptions.
A small number of northern and Baltic states — Estonia, Ireland, Latvia, Finland, Sweden — are holding stronger overall stock positions and are partly responsible for flattering the EU-wide averages.
